[/ˈdiːs/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") is a small community in the [Cassiar Country](/wiki/Cassiar_Country "Cassiar Country") of the [Northern Interior of British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia_Interior "British Columbia Interior"), Canada. It is 230 km south of the [Yukon](/wiki/Yukon "Yukon") border on [Stewart–Cassiar Highway](/wiki/Stewart%E2%80%93Cassiar_Highway "Stewart–Cassiar Highway") (Highway 37) at the south end of the [lake of the same name](/wiki/Dease_Lake_(British_Columbia) "Dease Lake (British Columbia)"). Dease Lake is the last major centre before the [Alaska Highway](/wiki/Alaska_Highway "Alaska Highway") while driving northbound, and also the junction to [Telegraph Creek](/wiki/Telegraph_Creek "Telegraph Creek") and the [Grand Canyon of the Stikine](/wiki/Grand_Canyon_of_the_Stikine "Grand Canyon of the Stikine"). [Dease Lake Indian Reserve No. 9](/wiki/Dease_River_First_Nation "Dease River First Nation") is nearby and is under the governance of the [Tahltan First Nation](/wiki/Tahltan_First_Nation "Tahltan First Nation") band government.

**Dease Lake** [/ˈdiːs/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") is a small community in the [Cassiar Country](/wiki/Cassiar_Country "Cassiar Country") of the [Northern Interior of British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia_Interior "British Columbia Interior"), Canada. It is 230 km south of the [Yukon](/wiki/Yukon "Yukon") border on [Stewart–Cassiar Highway](/wiki/Stewart%E2%80%93Cassiar_Highway "Stewart–Cassiar Highway") (Highway 37) at the south end of the [lake of the same name](/wiki/Dease_Lake_(British_Columbia) "Dease Lake (British Columbia)"). Dease Lake is the last major centre before the [Alaska Highway](/wiki/Alaska_Highway "Alaska Highway") while driving northbound, and also the junction to [Telegraph Creek](/wiki/Telegraph_Creek "Telegraph Creek") and the [Grand Canyon of the Stikine](/wiki/Grand_Canyon_of_the_Stikine "Grand Canyon of the Stikine"). [Dease Lake Indian Reserve No. 9](/wiki/Dease_River_First_Nation "Dease River First Nation") is nearby and is under the governance of the [Tahltan First Nation](/wiki/Tahltan_First_Nation "Tahltan First Nation") band government.

The town sits astride a drainage divide separating the basins of the [Dease River](/wiki/Dease_River "Dease River") (to the north) from that of the Tanzilla (to the south), a tributary of the [Stikine](/wiki/Stikine_River "Stikine River"). As this is a division point between drainage to the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean"), via the Stikine, and the [Arctic Ocean](/wiki/Arctic_Ocean "Arctic Ocean"), via the [Liard](/wiki/Liard_River "Liard River") and [Mackenzie Rivers](/wiki/Mackenzie_River "Mackenzie River"), this is part of the [Continental Divide](/wiki/Continental_Divide_of_the_Americas "Continental Divide of the Americas").

The town has a school, various stores, a fuel and service station, hotel, and a [Northern Lights College](/wiki/Northern_Lights_College "Northern Lights College") campus. It used to have a restaurant and a pub, but both have closed. The town sees a large influx of visitors during the summer months from tourists on their way to the [Alaska Highway](/wiki/Alaska_Highway "Alaska Highway"), [Yukon](/wiki/Yukon "Yukon"), and [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska"). A majority of these tourists are from Canada or the United States. Dease Lake is also a destination for hunting and other wilderness activities, and the local economy benefits from local gold, copper, and jade mining and exploration activities.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dease_Lake&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

In 1837 a [Hudson's Bay Company](/wiki/Hudson%27s_Bay_Company "Hudson's Bay Company") post, known as [Lake House](/wiki/Lake_House "Lake House"), was created by [Robert Campbell](/wiki/Robert_Campbell_(fur_trader) "Robert Campbell (fur trader)") on the shore of Dease Lake about 50 km (31 mi) north of the [Stikine River](/wiki/Stikine_River "Stikine River") and 150 km (93 mi) south of where the present day [Alaska Highway](/wiki/Alaska_Highway "Alaska Highway") passes. The Lake had been named in 1834 for [Chief Factor](/wiki/Factor_(agent) "Factor (agent)") [Peter Warren Dease](/wiki/Peter_Warren_Dease "Peter Warren Dease"), and would become a major junction for miners travelling to the gold rush in [Cassiar](/wiki/Cassiar,_British_Columbia "Cassiar, British Columbia") (later an asbestos mine). Although the fort was abandoned soon after, the town based around the fort lived on, and was renamed Dease Lake in 1934 by then-Chief Trader John McLeod.

During the 1960s and 1970s, [BC Rail](/wiki/BC_Rail "BC Rail") started to build an extension of their line towards Dease Lake, but construction was halted. Grading was completed all the way, and can still be seen from the air at [58°03′N 129°49′W﻿ / ﻿58.050°N 129.817°W﻿ / 58.050; -129.817﻿ (Dease Lake railbed diverging from Hwy 37)](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Dease_Lake&params=58_03_N_129_49_W_&title=Dease+Lake+railbed+diverging+from+Hwy+37).

## Geography and climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dease_Lake&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography and climate")]

North of Dease Lake is [Good Hope Lake](/wiki/Good_Hope_Lake "Good Hope Lake") (138 km [86 mi]) and the [Alaska Highway](/wiki/Alaska_Highway "Alaska Highway") (235 km [146 mi]). South of Dease Lake is [Iskut](/wiki/Iskut "Iskut") (65 km [40 mi]), [Stewart](/wiki/Stewart,_British_Columbia "Stewart, British Columbia") (398 km [247 mi]), and [Kitwanga](/wiki/Kitwanga "Kitwanga") (489 km [304 mi]).

Dease Lake has a [subarctic climate](/wiki/Subarctic_climate "Subarctic climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") *Dfc*), typical of northern British Columbia. Summers are mild, coupled with chilly nights while winters are severely cold and snowy, with annual snowfall averaging 212.8 cm (83.78 in). Autumn typically begins by early September and lasts into October when winter begins. Winter can last into late March or sometimes early April. Spring usually lasts until late May or early June, after when summer begins. Due to Dease Lake's high elevation, snowfall is possible during any month of the year.
